text:
It makes sense that you feel this way. Homesickness is one of the most disorienting feelings a person can experience, partly because it catches so many people off guard. You expect to feel excitement about wherever you are now, and instead you feel this hollow ache for something familiar, and that disconnect can make it worse.

What you are missing is real. It is not weakness, and it is not a sign that you made a wrong decision. Home is where your nervous system learned to relax. It is the sound of a specific street, the way light comes through a window you have known your whole life, the particular comfort of people who already know you without explanation. Of course your body and heart reach for that.

The first time is the hardest precisely because you have no evidence yet that you will be okay. You have never done this before, so you cannot reassure yourself from experience. But almost everyone who has ever felt what you are feeling right now eventually found their way through it, and most of them would tell you that they came out the other side with something they did not have before, a kind of confidence, a deeper knowledge of themselves, a relationship with home that actually deepened because they learned what it meant to them.

Give yourself permission to miss it. Do not fight the feeling or tell yourself you should be over it. Let yourself call home, look at old photos, eat familiar food if you can find it. These are not signs of failure. They are how you stay connected while you build something new.

And try to give the new place a fair chance, even in small ways. Not because it will replace home, but because somewhere inside you there is more capacity than you know. Home was once unfamiliar too, before you loved it.

You will get through this. And one day, sooner than it feels possible right now, you will have two places that feel like yours.
